coredata.dc:title: Portable smartphone platform based on a ratio fluorescence probe for situ visual monition of cardiac disease markers in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Shanshan
Zhang, Hongyuan
Wang, Chenzhao
Yin, Xiangyu
Hu, Kaixin
liu, Min
Jiang, Chunzhu
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: Creatine kinase (CK), as an important biochemical index of cardiac injury, is very significant for early and accurate diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction or cardiac arrest. However, the sensitivity and specificity of CK detection still face great challenges. Herein, a ratio fluorescence probe was designed and prepared by electrostatic interaction between boron and nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-L-CDs) as a response signal and Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as independent reference signal. Notably, the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ displayed an ultra-fast fluorescence response to CK in ATP medium and an excellent linear relationship was observed between the concentration of CK and the fluorescence recovery ratio with a detection limit as low as 1.20 U/L. Mechanism investigates demonstrated that the “ON-OFF-ON” progress could be attributed to the formation of the non-luminescent ground state complex BN-L-CDs-ATP by the interaction of BN-L-CDs with ATP and the dissociation of complex under the catalysis of CK, respectively. Besides, the ratio fluorescence probe was further applied to living cell imaging due to its excellent biocompatibility. Importantly, a portable visual sensing platform was developed by combining a ratio fluorescence probe with a mobile phone color recognition application. Therefore, this new sensor provided a new perspective for the convenient monitoring of CK and paved the way for the relevant applications of clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Ratio fluorescent probe
CK
AMI and smartphone

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ATP and CK determination in real samples
Characterization of the ratio fluorescent probe
Detection of ATP and CK by BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+
Smartphone application to detect ATP and CK
Detection of ATP and CK in living cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of BN-L-CDs, inset: particle size distribution, (b) HR-TEM image of BN-L-CDs. (c) AFM image and (d) XRD pattern of BN-L-CDs. (e) XPS full spectrum of BN-L-CDs. the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (f), N1s (g), O1s (h) and B1s (i).

(a) The UV–vis absorbance spectrum and the Ex and Em spectra; Inset: the BN-L-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right), (b) emission spectra, (c) 3D fluorescence emission spectra and (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of BN-L-CDs.

(a) The selectivity of BN-L-CDs, (b) the fluorescence intensity variation of BN-L-CDs in presence of ATP, (c) the relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and different ATP concentrations (d) the competitive selectivity of BN-L-CDs-ATP system, (e) the fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs-ATP system in different concentrations of CK, (f) the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CK.

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra (ATP), and excitation and emission spectra of BN-L-CDs, (b) fluorescence decay curve, (c) the linear range of (F0-F)/F0 at different ATP concentrations, (d) the UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-L-CDs, ATP, BN-L-CDs + ATP and BN-L-CDs + ATP + CK, (e) the XPS spectra of BN-L-CDs + ATP and BN-L-CDs + ATP + CK, (f) the XPS spectra of BN-L-CDs and BN-L-CDs + ATP, (g) the FT-IR spectra of BN-L-CDs, BN-L-CDs + ATP and BN-L-CDs + ATP + CK (h) the zeta potentials of BN-L-CDs, ATP, BN-L-CDs + ATP and BN-L-CDs + ATP + CK, respectively, (i) the schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching and recovery process.

Characterization of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+, (a) fluorescence emission spectra of Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O upon addition of ATP, (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (c) XPS and (d) Zeta-potential of BN-L-CDs, Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O and BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+.

(a) Fluorescence variation of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ in presence of ATP, (b) the line relation between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of ATP, (c) ratio fluorescence probe with ATP in presence of competing substances, (d) the fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+with different concentrations of CK, (e) linear relationship between the FL intensity and the concentration of CK, (f) the selectivity of the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+-ATP for the detection of CK, (g) reversibility research of the ratio fluorescence probe between ATP and CK, (h) color change of ratio fluorescent probe in presence of ATP (i) the procedure for detecting CK.
the schematic illustration of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for visual detection of CK.

Evaluation of the nuclear localization of BN-L-CDs in 4T1 cells. (a,b) 4T1 cells co-incubated with BN-L-CDs and Hoechst 33342. (c) Plot analysis of ROI. Green line: BN-L-CDs, blue line: Hoechst 33342. (d) Images of BN-L-CDs, BN-L-CDs + ATP and BN-L-CDs + ATP + CK. (e) 4T1 cells incubated with ratio fluorescence probe and exogenous ATP and CK, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ as an efficient ratio fluorescent probe for the fluorescence and visual detection of ATP and CK, respectively.

head.ce:title: Portable smartphone platform based on a ratio fluorescence probe for situ visual monition of cardiac disease markers in vitro

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creatine kinase (CK), as an important biochemical index of cardiac injury, is very significant for early and accurate diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction or cardiac arrest. However, the sensitivity and specificity of CK detection still face great challenges. Herein, a ratio fluorescence probe was designed and prepared by electrostatic interaction between boron and nitrogen doped carbon dots (BN-L-CDs) as a response signal and Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as independent reference signal. Notably, the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ displayed an ultra-fast fluorescence response to CK in ATP medium and an excellent linear relationship was observed between the concentration of CK and the fluorescence recovery ratio with a detection limit as low as 1.20 U/L. Mechanism investigates demonstrated that the “ON-OFF-ON” progress could be attributed to the formation of the non-luminescent ground state complex BN-L-CDs-ATP by the interaction of BN-L-CDs with ATP and the dissociation of complex under the catalysis of CK, respectively. Besides, the ratio fluorescence probe was further applied to living cell imaging due to its excellent biocompatibility. Importantly, a portable visual sensing platform was developed by combining a ratio fluorescence probe with a mobile phone color recognition application. Therefore, this new sensor provided a new perspective for the convenient monitoring of CK and paved the way for the relevant applications of clinical diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) or cardiac arrest caused by acute or persistent coronary artery ischemia and hypoxia is currently one of the deadliest diseases in the world, which brings a great challenge for the medical community [1,2]. Timely diagnosis and immediate reperfusion therapy are desired to potentially reduce mortality [3,4]. Creatine kinase (CK), myocardial myoglobin and troponin are effective biomarkers for clinical diagnosis of AMI patients [4]. Among them, CK is one of the most mature biomarkers of heart injury with the clinical concomitant rate of 90 % [3,5,6]. The key role of CK is to catalyze creatine (Cr) to form phosphocreatine (CrP) while converting ATP to ADP and inorganic phosphate [6–8]. Generally, the serum CK level of a normal human is in the low concentration range of 45–174 U/L. However, CK is released from the myocardium into the circulation, resulting in a significant rise in serum CK levels as early as 4 h after coronary artery occlusion [3,9]. Hence, it can be applied as an meaningful biomarker for the early diagnosis of AMI. In fact, several kinds of analysis methods for detecting CK activity have been developed such as ELISA, chemiluminescence, and enzyme immunoassay [10]. However, these approaches still suffer from limitations including experienced technicians, sophisticated instruments and cumbersome procedures [11,12]. Therefore, it is crucial to overcome these limitations to prepare sensitive and practical biosensors for the early detection of CK in clinical applications [3,13,14].

With the emergence of fluorescent materials, fluorescence probes have been widely applied in the field of analytical detection due to the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, and continuous detection [15,16]. Unfortunately, the traditional single-emission fluorescent probes are disturbed by strong biological backgrounds and environmental fluctuations [11]. On the contrary, the ratio fluorescence probe as a novel analytical technique can effectively eliminate the adverse effects of excitation source fluctuations, local environmental changes, and the variation of probe concentration [17–19]. Moreover, the ratio fluorescence probe not only has internal self-calibration capability to improve the accuracy of the detection system, but also possesses significant color changes to achieve better visual detection on the basis of wide color changes [11,12]. Generally, the ratio fluorescence sensors are constructed by combining two components with different emission wavelengths, in which one component plays the role of improving accuracy and stability and the other is responsible for detecting the analyte [12,20]. The components employed to construct ratio fluorescence probe include single-emission carbon dots (CDs), polymer dots, upconversion nanoprobes, metal clusters, metal–organic frames, fluorescent molecules, and fluorescent proteins [19]. Notably, CDs as a novel class of zero-dimensional material have received great attention from many researchers in the construction of ratio fluorescent sensors due to their unique optical stability, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, convenient surface modification, and uniform size distribution [15,17]. For example, Zhang and his colleagues prepared an ultra-sensitive ratio fluorescence probe using sulfonamide derivatives to monitor the excessive glutathione (GSH) in the mitochondria [11]. Chen and his colleagues designed an efficient ratio fluorescent probe for the detection of formaldehyde (FA) in lysosomes of HeLa cells [17]. Zhou and his colleagues reported a novel ratio probe based on Eu-CDs for colorimetric and fluorescent visual sensing the pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA) [20]. Unfortunately, these ratio fluorescent probes are only suitable for detection under laboratory conditions, which severely limits their application in real-time or on-site detection [12]. Therefore, to accurately and conveniently track various analytes, it is necessary to convert optical and visual information of fluorescence into data information to realize rapid and real-time field analysis and detection targets [12,16].

In this work, the boron and nitrogen-doped chiral carbon dots (BN-L/D-CDs) were prepared via the one-step solvothermal approach using 4-carboxyphenylboric acid (4-PBA) and L/D-aspartic acid (L/D-Asp) as precursors (Fig. S1). The as-prepared BN-L/D-CDs possessed bright green fluorescence, excellent water solubility and optical stability. A novel ratio fluorescent probe (BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+) was designed by introducing Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as independent reference signal, which could efficaciously shield interference toward matrix (Scheme 1 ). The ratio fluorescent probes could selectively and sensitively detect ATP and CK under optimal conditions. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of BN-L-CDs could be explained as the binding of 4-PBA groups to the cis diol portion of ATP molecules, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Subsequently, when creatine was added to the BN-L-CDs system and catalyzed by CK, the ATP was converted into ADP and inorganic phosphate, inducing the fluorescence recovery. In addition, the low cytotoxicity and excellent ATP and CK response characteristics of the ratio fluorescent probes were also confirmed in living cells. Interestingly, the RGB software could be employed for CK quantitative detection by analyzing the ratio of red (R), green (G) and blue (B) channels of the images. Therefore, this portable sensor platform not only provides a new strategy for CK detection but also lays a foundation for building a reliable and convenient detection platform.

The introduction of heteroatoms and the selection of precursors are important factors for the fluorescence modulation of CDs [21]. Generally, the doped elements of CDs are mainly nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) due to the synergistic effect of N atom and the low electronegativity of S atom [22,23]. Interestingly, the electronic structure and optical properties of CDs could change when boron (B) atom as a typical P-type carrier was introduced into CDs [24]. Recently, it has been reported that CDs doped with B could not only significantly enhance the nonlinear optical response of CDs but also increase the quantum yield of CDs [25]. Moreover, L/D-Aspartic acid (L/D-Asp) as one of the acidic amino acids plays a key role in biosynthesis [14,23]. Thus, L/D-Asp as carbon source could endow CDs not only excellent biocompatibility but also chiral function. The BN-L/D-CDs were prepared by one-step solvothermal method with 4-carboxyphenylboric acid, acetamide and L/D-Asp as precursors (Fig. S1). Compared with the CDs without L/D-Asp, the cyan blue fluorescence of BN-L/D-CDs at 486 nm was significantly enhanced (Fig. S2a and b). Moreover, the strongest fluorescence intensity of these BN-L/D-CDs could be obtained when the molar mass ratio of the precursor, the reaction temperature and the reaction time was 1:1:4, 200℃, and 6 h (Fig. S3a–c). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of BN-L-CDs (11.73 %) was higher than that of BN-D-CDs (8.37 %) [26,27]. Therefore, most of the features described below apply to BN-L-CDs as examples [14,28].

The morphology and size distribution of BN-L-CDs was observed by TEM and AFM [29,30]. These CDs possessed spherical morphology and excellent dispersion with the average size of 11.32 ± 0.35 nm (Fig. 1 a) [17]. The HR-TEM images displayed a distinct fringe spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), matching with the (100) lattice distance of graphitic carbon, which demonstrated that the BN-L-CDs possessed a highly crystalline carbon structure [18]. Moreover, the topographic height of BN-L-CDs displayed that the thickness of the BN-L-CDs was about 3.90 nm, which was much smaller than their diameter indicating that the prepared CDs were layered (Fig. 1c) [14,19]. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, there was a broad peak near 22.53° in the XRD pattern of BN-L-CDs, suggesting that the carbon atoms were highly disordered due to the weak interaction between the layers in the structure of carbon quantum dots [19,20,23]. Moreover, the Raman spectra displayed two obvious absorption peaks of D-band and G-band (Fig. S4), corresponding to the sp3 hybrid carbon and sp2 carbon domains in carbon materials [31]. Then, the functional groups of BN-L-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectra [23]. The wide band at 3100–3500 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H (Fig. S5) [21]. The absorption bands at 1660 cm−1, 1560 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, 1370 cm−1, and 1150 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibrations of CO, CC, BO, BN and BC bonds [12,23,32]. Subsequently, the valence state information of the elements for BN-L-CDs was explored by XPS [33]. The full range XPS spectrum contained C, O, N, and B with the percentages of each element of 70.25 %, 24.68 %, 3.95 % and 1.12 %, respectively (Fig. 1e) [28,34]. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s was fitted into three peaks at 284.60 eV (C = C/C − C), 286.20 eV (C–O) and COOH groups (288.50 eV), respectively (Fig. 1f) [18,23,35]. The N1s spectrum displayed three peaks at 398.62 eV, 399.92 eV and 401.60 eV (Fig. 1g), which could be attributed to B–N, N–H and C–N [14,23]. The high-resolution B1s spectrum was deconvolved to three peaks of 189.20 eV (B–C), 191.60 eV (B–N) and 192.50 eV (B–O), respectively (Fig. 1i) [1]. Lastly, a new peak at 532.50 eV was observed in the O 1 s spectrum, demonstrating the presence of BO bond (Fig. 1h) [14,35]. Therefore, the XPS and the FT-IR spectra results suggested that the elements of B and N were successfully doped [21,23,25].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of BN-L-CDs were explored [27]. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, a wide absorbance peak from 200 nm to 300 nm was contributed to the π → π* transition of C = C [9]. The absorption peak at 320 nm was ascribed to n → π* transition of C = O of BN-L-CDs [24,33]. Then, the fluorescence emission spectrum with and without boron was investigated. As illustrated in inset of Fig. 2a and Fig. S6, the B-doped CDs (BN-L-CDs) displayed a bright cyan fluorescence with a maximum emission peak at 486 nm while the non-doped CDs possessed weak green emission at 506 nm [14,25]. Compared to the non-doped CDs the emission blue shift of the B-doped CDs was ascribed to the strong electron-withdrawing character of boron [24]. And the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the B-doped CDs was 11.73 %, higher than 9.89 % of the un-doped CDs (Fig. S7) [24,27]. Subsequently, the steady-state fluorescence spectra of BN-L-CDs displayed excitation dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 2b and 2c), which might be related to surface defects [15,33]. To determine the color of emission wavelengths, the CIE chromaticity diagrams of BN-L-CDs were collected. As displayed in Fig. 2d, the CIE coordinate of BN-L-CDs was (0.28, 0.27), indicating extraordinary color purity of BN-L-CDs in the cyan region. Moreover, the circular dichroism (CD) spectra of BN-L/D-CDs and L/D-Asp were recorded, respectively. The L/D-Asp possessed CD signals at + 200 nm and −200 nm (Fig. S8a), while the CD signals of the BN-L/D-CDs extended from 200 nm to 220 nm, suggesting that the precursors underwent carbonization and polymerization [14,31]. A new CD signal appeared at 203 nm, which was attributed to the π → π* transition of the sp2-hybrid carbon structure in BN-L/D-CDs (Fig. S8b) [26]. Thus, these results indicated that the chiral and achiral precursors bonded specifically during solvothermal processes, and that the chiral characteristics of BN-L/D-CDs were partially originated in the structure of the product itself [14,27,31].

The fluorescence stability of BN-L-CDs in a complex biological environment were explored [27,36]. There scarcely displayed the fluorescence fluctuation of BN-L-CDs with diverse temperatures, concentrations of NaCl, H2O2 and UV irradiation time (Fig. S9a–9c and 9e). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs was relatively stable in the pH value range from 4.0 to 10.0, and there was a significant downward trend in the strong acid (1.0 to 3.0) or alkali environment (11.0 to 14.0) (Fig. S9d). The phenomenon of pH dependence was attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of BN-L-CDs probably due to the presence of nitrogen atoms at the BN-L-CDs [37,38].Thereafter, the BN-L-CDs displayed excellent stability in various organic solvents such as methanol (MT), cyclohexane (CYH), toluene (TL), ethanol (EA), acetone (CP), N, N, 2-methylformamide (DMF) and (Fig. S9f). Briefly, the as-prepared BN-L-CDs possessed excellent photostability and could be applied in complex biological environments [9,22].

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) participates in various metabolic processes and holds a unique position in nature [27,39]. The fluctuation of ATP level is closely related to ischemia, malignant tumors and cardiovascular diseases [9]. As a consequence, real-time and in-situ monitoring of ATP content in living tissues is of great significance [15]. Firstly, the influences of potential interfering substances including cation ions (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+ and Fe2+) and other small molecules (L-Phe, L-Lle, L-Asp, L-Glu, L-Try, ClO−, NO2 −, H2O2, H2S, SO3 −, AMP, ADP and ATP) were evaluated [33]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, only the addition of ATP could quench the fluorescence of BN-L-CDs, while the influence of others could be ignored, suggesting that the above mentioned interfering species had no great influence on the ATP detection [32]. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity at 486 nm declined after the addition of ATP, indicating that the BN-L-CDs could sensitively detect ATP molecule (Fig. 3b) [19]. More importantly, the ATP could quench 80 % of the fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs within 5 min (Fig. S10a) [26]. An excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.991, P > F = 4.441E-16) between the concentration of ATP (10–100 µM) and the value of (F0-F)/F0 were obtained, and the detection limit (LOD) were determined to be 18.06 nM (Fig. 3c) [35]. Compared with the previously reported methods for ATP detection (Table S1), this work had many advantages including low detection limit, simple operation and good biocompatibility [10,36]. Moreover, CK plays a core role in the coordination of intracellular energy supply and demand and is usually served as an important biochemical indicator of cardiac injury for the early and accurate diagnosis of AMI or cardiac arrest [4,6,7]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a sensitive biosensor for early detection of CK in clinical applications. Herein, the fluorescence response behavior of the BN-L-CDs towards different concentrations of CK was evaluated under the coexistence of ATP and creatine (Cr) [9,33]. Initially, the interference species included glucose (Glu), trypsin (Try), vitamin C (VC), uric acid (UA), arginine (Arg), lysine (Lys), ZnCl2, NaCl, KCl, and CK were considered under the same conditions. It could be observed from Fig. 3d that the interference of these substances on BN-L-CD in the control group could be neglected. The fluorescence recovered completely after adding CK for 20 min (Fig. S10b) [9]. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs was positively correlated with the concentration of CK solution in an S-shaped manner (Fig. 3e). Particularly, the recovered fluorescence intensity of BN-L-CDs possessed an excellent linear correlation (y = 1.085x + 492.111 (R2 = 0.994, P > F = 4.428E-6) with CK concentration in the range of 0–150 U/L (Fig. 3f). And the detection limit (LOD) was determined as 1.20 U/L, suggesting that the BN-L-CDs-ATP system had high sensitivity and feasibility for CK detecting.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms included dynamic quenching and static quenching [23]. The former is due to collisional reaction between the excited molecules of the luminous substance and the quencher, while the latter is a non-luminous ground state complex formed between the luminous substance and the quencher [14]. There was no obvious overlap between the excitation or emission spectra of BN-L-CDs and the UV–vis absorbance of ATP aqueous solution (Fig. 4 a), which indicated that the possibility of internal filtration effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for the BN-L-CDs-ATP system was excluded [32]. Then, the UV–vis absorption peak of BN-L-CDs in the presence of ATP was explored. A new absorption peak appeared at 258 nm and gradually increased with the increase of ATP concentration (Fig. 4d), suggesting that new complex formed [9,26]. Subsequently, the changes of transient fluorescence spectra before and after the introduction of ATP in BN-L-CDs were collected [25,33]. The fluorescence lifetime of BN-L-CDs (0.90 ns) barely fluctuated after adding ATP (0.96 ns) (Fig. 4b), which further indicated that the static quenching occurred [9,21,39]. Moreover, the Stern-Volmer diagram was also employed to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Fig. 4c).
(1)
F 0
/ F - 1 =
K q
τ 0
Q
=
K
sv
Q

F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity with and without ATP, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of ATP. Ksv and Kq is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the quenching rate constant, respectively. τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of BN-L-CDs [33]. τ0 was determined as 0.90 ns by instantaneous fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 4b). Kq was calculated as 2.6 × 1012 M−1 s−1, higher than the dynamic quenching rate constant (1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1), demonstrating that the static quenching happened (Fig. 4c) [23]. In addition, the quenching process before and after adding ATP was investigated by FT-IR, XPS spectra and zeta potential. As described in Fig. 4g, the FT-IR spectra displayed that the stretching vibration of BO bond at 1410 cm−1 enhanced in the presence of ATP, suggesting that the BN-L-CDs did complex with the cis-diol structure of the ATP [40]. Subsequently, the XPS spectra were employed to further confirm the results of the formation of complexes between BN-L-CDs and ATP. When ATP was added to the BN-L-CDs system, a significant decline in the peak intensity of B1s could be observed, which suggested that electrons of B and O elements participated in the coordination of ATP, and carboxyl and amino groups as active sites involved in the recognition of ATP (Fig. 4f) [40]. Moreover, the high-resolution P2p spectrum displayed two peaks of P-O (133.10 eV) and P-C/P-N (135.20 eV) (Fig. 4e), further demonstrating the non-luminous ground state complex formed between the BN-L-CDs and ATP [19]. The above data confirmed that the fluorescence quenching mode of BN-L-CDs in the presence of ATP could be ascribed to static quenching.

The reason for fluorescence enhancement was that CK induced ATP convert to ADP in the presence of Cr, and the BN-L-CDs-ATP complex was decomposed, leading to fluorescence recovery [2,5,7]. Subsequently, the fluorescence recovery mechanism of BN-L-CDs-ATP system was further investigated by Zeta-potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS). The Zeta potential of BN-L-CDs decreased from −0.47 mV to −13.80 mV after adding ATP (Fig. 4h), which could be interpreted as ATP partially shielding the positive amino group of CDs [15]. Additionally, the potential changed to −23.0 mV in the presence of Cr and CK, which was higher than the mixed solution of BN-L-CDs and ATP, resulting in fluorescence recovery [19,39]. Further, DLS data displayed that the hydrodynamic diameter of the BN-L-CDs-ATP complex decreased from 130 nm to 50 nm after CK addition (Fig. S12c and d), indicating that the non-luminous ground state complex was dissociated [5]. Moreover, the UV–Vis absorption peak declined due to the complex formation, which reduced the energy of the entire BN-CDs-ATP system and made the electronic transition of
π
→ π
∗
and n →
π
∗
easier to realize [24,35]. Likewise, the fluorescence restoration process was further explored by XPS spectra. As described in Fig. 4e, the characteristic peaks of P-O (133.10 eV) declined in the high-resolution P2p spectrum when creatine and CK were added to the BN-L-CDs-ATP system, which suggested that the BN-L-CDs-ATP complex dissociated under the catalysis of CK [33]. Therefore, the increase of CK content in the detection system could induce the conversion of ATP to ADP, reducing the quenching effect of fluorescent CDs by ATP and then promoting fluorescence recovery (Fig. 4i) [9,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ATP and CK determination in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of BN-L-CDs for sensing ATP and CK in real samples was evaluated [21,41]. BSA solution and mouse serum were selected as complex environment to detect ATP and CK by standard addition method [12]. The recovery rates of ATP and CK in the real samples were 87.92–106.00 % and 96.10–109.00 % (Table S2), respectively. The above results demonstrated that this ratio probe was accurate and reliable and could be applied to detect ATP and CK in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the ratio fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para:
Ratio fluorescence probe is based on detecting the ratio of fluorescence intensity at two well-defined wavelengths, so it can effectively eliminate the influence of excitation source fluctuation, local environmental change and probe concentration change [12,19], and then greatly improve the accuracy of detection. In this work, a sensitive ratio fluorescence probe of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for the detection of ATP and CK was designed by employing negatively charged BN-L-CDs as a response unit and positively charged Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as an internal reference fluorescence signal [15,16]. As demonstrated by Fig. 5 a, the high concentrations of ATP had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O, indicating that Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O was very suitable as an internal reference fluorescence molecule for the preparation of ratio fluorescence probes [16]. Then, the combination of BN-L-CDs and Ru (bpy)3Cl2·6H2O was investigated by measuring the UV–vis, FT-IR and XPS spectra [21,35]. The obvious characteristic peaks at 450 nm of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ were attributed to the n
→
π* transition of CO double bonds of Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O (Fig. 5b) [40]. Moreover, the out-of-plane bending vibration of aromatic hydrogen appeared at 900–700 cm−1, which belonged to pyridine aromatic heterocyclic rings in Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O (Fig. S11) [15,22,28]. The structural composition of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ was further characterized by XPS. As described in Fig. 5c and Fig. S13, the high-resolution spectrum of Ru3p was deconvolved to two peaks at 462.20 eV (Ru3p3/2) and 484.37 eV (Ru3p1/2), suggesting that Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O was successfully combined with BN-L-CDs. Subsequently, the Zeta potentials of BN-L-CDs, Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O and BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ probe were −0.47 mV, 7.06 mV, and 2.20 mV, respectively (Fig. 5d), which demonstrated that the successful connected between BN-L-CDs and Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O via electrostatic interaction [1,12]. The dynamic light scattering size distribution and TEM demonstrated that the size of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ increased from 30 nm to 50 nm (Fig. S12a and b), which further confirmed that the ratio fluorescent probe of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ was successfully developed [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ATP and CK by BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the ratio fluorescence probe for the detection of ATP and CK was investigated [15]. When ATP solutions with incremental concentration were introduced to the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ system, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually at 486 nm while the reference signal remained unchanged at 600 nm (Fig. 6 a) [19]. An excellent linear relationship between the concentrations of ATP (0–25 µM) and the (F0-F)/F0 was fitted, and the LOD could be calculated as 13.80 nM (R2 = 0.999, P > F = 2.474E-5) (Fig. 6b). Moreover, the color of the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ solution under the same condition gradually altered from cyan to red with the increase of ATP concentration (Fig. S14b). Subsequently, the selectivity of the sensor was evaluated by different kinds of distractions including metal ions (1–5: Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+ and Ca2+), amino acids (6–10: L-Phe, L-Lle, L-Asp, L-Glu, and L-Lys) and adenosine triphosphate homologues (11–15: Na5P3O10, Na3PO4, AMP and ADP) [3,9]. As described in Fig. 6c, the influence of interfering substances on the ratio fluorescence probe was negligible except for the homologues of ATP due to their similar structure [15]. Therefore, these experimental results indicated that the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ as an effective fluorescent probe could selectively detect ATP [18,21]. Moreover, the response of BN-L-CDs-ATP system toward CK was illustrated in Fig. 6d. The fluorescence intensity of the BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ could be effectively restored after adding CK for 20 min with an s-type positive correlation and the LOD was 1.32 U/L (R2 = 0.996, P > F = 7.572E-12) (Fig. 6e). Moreover, the solution color of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+-ATP system gradually changed from red to cyan with the increase of CK concentration (Fig. 6h). Subsequently, a series of control experiments were conducted under the same conditions. As described in Fig. 6f, the effects of bovine serum albumin (BSA) and other small molecules or ions on the BN-L-CDs-ATP system could also be ignored [9]. Notably, the ratio fluorescence probe could be repeatedly switched between ATP and CK, indicating that the recognition process was reversible (Fig. 6g) [28]. As expected, the fluorescence probe had high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of CK activity in complex biological substrates (Fig. 6i) [3,9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application to detect ATP and CK
ce:section.ce:para: Although commercial instruments can meet the requirements of quantitative analysis, the practicability and accuracy of portable instruments are of great significance in field quantitative detection [32,41,42]. The BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ displayed color transform from green to red under 365 nm excitation as ATP concentration increased. Nevertheless, an accurate quantitative analysis could not be achieved due to the inability of the human eyes to distinguish color changes in detail [12,42]. To solve this problem, the quantitative ratio fluorescence sensing platform of ATP based on smart phones was constructed [41–43]. This strategy could effectively reduce the errors induced by environmental and operational factors [32]. And the color information of the photos was converted into RGB values, which were analyzed by the application of the color recognizer [12]. As illustrated in Fig. S14a and b, a series of photos were taken with a smart phone under the irradiation of 365 nm after the ratio fluorescence probe reacted with ATP solutions of different concentrations [34]. An excellent linear relationship between the red/green-channel (R/G) value with the different concentrations of ATP were required (Table S3 and Fig. S14c), and the LOD was calculated as 35.26 nM (R2 = 0.98, P > F = 8.033E-4). These results demonstrated that the intelligent sensor platform achieved the sensitive detection of ATP, which would provide a certain degree of technical support for public safety and life and health [43].

Subsequently, the fluorescence color of ratio fluorescence probe gradually changed from red to green with the CK concentration increasing from 0 to 300 U/L [44]. Therefore, the solution concentration was distinguished by color and divided into “not exceeding the standard”, “critical state” and “exceeding the standard”. Subsequently, a series of pictures were collected with a smart phone under 365 nm excitation and analyzed by color recognition software to obtain their RGB values (Table S4) [12]. An excellent linear relationship was received by calculating the value of R/G, which could be expressed as Y = -0.0046x + 2.039 (R2 = 0.9956, P > F = 1.98238E-4), and the LOD was determined as 1.65 U/L (Fig. 7 ) [44]. The above results fully demonstrated that the smart phone auxiliary sensing platform based on BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ could quickly realize the visualization and high sensitivity detection of ATP and CK, and significantly improve the detection efficiency [13,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ATP and CK in living cell
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of BN-L-CDs and BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ ratio fluorescence probes on 4 T1 cells and their potential biological applications were investigated by CCK8 method [8,28,30]. As illustrated in Fig. S15, the cells were co-incubated with BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ system at a concentration up to 800 µg mL−1 for 24 h. As expected, the cells possessed a high survival rate over 85 %, which indicated that the ratio fluorescent probes with low cytotoxicity and could be further applied in cell imaging [14,15]. Next, the feasibility of ratio fluorescence probe for sensing ATP and CK in 4 T1 cells was verified by inverted fluorescence microscope [19,22]. Initially, the cells were treated with different concentrations of BN-L-CDs at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 to determine the optimal concentration [18,28]. As displayed in Fig. S16, the fluorescence intensity was optimal when the concentration was 100 μg mL−1. Subsequently, the fluorescence reached the strongest when the incubation time was 5 h as well as the cells exhibited blue and green fluorescence in the UV–vis and blue channel (Fig. S17), suggesting that these BN-L-CDs could cross the cell membrane favorably through endocytosis into the cells [14,19,44]. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a–8c, the Hoechst 33,342 was well localized to the nucleus and the BN-L-CDs only distributed in the cytoplasm [14]. Therefore, the above experimental results demonstrated that the BN-L-CDs did not possess the ability to locate the nucleus [14,25]. Subsequently, the imaging photos were collected when exogenous analysis was introduced. As described in Fig. 8d and 8e, the green emission displayed obvious quenching and recovery processes after adding exogenous ATP and CK to the BN-L-CDs and BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ system, respectively [7,15]. Therefore, the ratio fluorescence probe as an “on–off-on” nanosensor could be applied in the intracellular monitoring and imaging of CK.

In summary, a sensitive ratio fluorescence probe of BN-L-CDs@[Ru(bpy)3]2+ was successfully developed using BN-L-CDs as a response signal and Ru (bpy)3Cl2·6H2O as a reference signal. The ratio fluorescent probe possessed excellent optical properties, high selectivity, good biocompatibility, and stability in complex biological environments. Importantly, the ratio fluorescence probe not only obviously improved the stability of the detection system but also made visual detection possible. In addition, the accuracy of the proposed strategy was confirmed by measuring the content of ATP and CK in BSA and serum samples. Interestingly, a portable sensor platform was designed to recognize the corresponding color information through the color recognition APP for quantitative detection. As a consequence, the smartphone-based sensor platform provides a promising strategy for CK field monitoring, which has potential application value in heart disease prevention and diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jilin Province Natural Science Foundation (20220101350JC) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2241387).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.145614.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
